---
title: "Individual assignment 2"
date: 2026-04-17
---

# Set up

1. Fill in your name in the YAML of the `individual-assignment-template.qmd` file.
2. Read in your packages.  
3. Read in your data.  
4. Copy paste any code you need from class into your assignment script.

:::{.callout-note title="Practice your own editorial judgement!" collapse="true"}
You will not need _all_ the code from class to do your assignment.  

You will need to pick and choose the components that are relevant to the assignment.  

If you copy-paste your entire class script into your homework, you will need to revise your assignment!
:::

5. Render to .pdf to make sure that you can render (if not, come ask for help!).
6. Start your assignment!

# Problems

## 1. Numbers of individuals

In the "Observations per species" section, we made figures showing the total counts per species for two eBird groups: Herons, Ibis, and Allies and Waterfowl.  

We did this by summarizing the data for each group separately, then creating two separate figures.  

In this section, you will use `facet_wrap()` to create a new figure.

### a. Prep your data

Create a new data frame called `bird_obs_count` from `birds_wy2025` summarizing the total counts per species in each of the _four_ eBird groups.  

You should be able to copy-paste the code from class and modify it to complete this question. For any line that you change, include an annotation explaining _how_ you changed that line.  

### b. Make a new figure

Use a combination of `ggplot()`, `geom_()` and `facet_wrap()` (along with any associated functions to make your plot look nicer) to create a plot with total count on the _x-axis_ and species on the _y-axis_ with separate panels for each eBird group.  

For full credit, be sure to:  

- label the x- and y-axes
- get rid of the space between the bottom of the column and the axis line
- use custom colors
- get rid of the legend (which is a redundant feature)
- make sure all your axis labels are clear and readable (there are multiple solutions to this, from wrapping the labels to flipping the axes - do what you think is best to make sure that _all_ the text is visible)

Annotate all your code to demonstrate your understanding of what you have written.  

:::{.callout-note title="Weird looking y-axis?" collapse=true}
If your y-axis looks off, pay attention to what species are included in each group. Does it make sense that there is an axis tick for "Mallard" under "Shorebirds"? (Hint: no.)  

To fix this, try running your code with additional arguments in your `facet_wrap()` like:  

- `scales = "free_x"`
- `scales = "free_y"`
- `scales = "free"`

to see how the axes might change. Choose the option that makes most sense for your figure.
:::

### c. Interpret the figure

Across all eBird groups:  

- Which species had the highest total count?
- Which species had the lowest total count?  

Use values from the data frame you made in part a OR visual components of the figure you made in part b to justify your claim.  

## 2. Waterfowl species turnover

Species turnover describes differences in the composition of species (i.e. which species are present or absent, and how their abundances differ).

While species richness gives you a sense of how many species are in a community, species turnover can tell you which species are contributing to differences in abundance or richness between seasons.  

### a. Revisit an old figure

Look back at class materials under the section "Seasonal differences in number of individuals". 

In 1-2 sentences, describe seasonal differences in total counts for _Waterfowl_.  

Use visual components of the plot to justify your interpretation.

### b. Write pseudocode

Write pseudocode to calculate the total count _per species_ in Waterfowl per season.  

You should end up with a data frame that looks something like this:

![](/assignments/images/individual-assignment-02/dataframe-example.png){fig-align="center" width="70%"}


### c. Code your pseudocode.

Write the code that corresponds to your pseudocode.  

At the end of the code chunk, write code to run `str()` on your object to display the structure.

### d. Make a visualization.

Make a visualization of individual counts on the y-axis and season on the x-axis.  
Fill the bars by _species_.  

Annotate all code to demonstrate your understanding of what you've written.  

Additionally, for full credit:  

- label the x-axis, y-axis, and legend
- include a title
- get rid of the space at the bottom of the count axis
- use custom colors from a palette package or otherwise
- use a different theme from the default

:::{.callout-note title="x-axis order looking off?" collapse=true}
Remember that water years start on October 1 of one year and end on September 30 of the next year. That means that a logical season order is fall --> winter --> spring --> summer.  

If your axis order needs fixing, set the levels in your code in part c.
:::

### e. Understanding trends

In 1-2 sentences each, describe:  

- What species is the most abundant across seasons?
- Which species increased in abundance when comparing fall to winter?
- Which species contribute to the increased _number of Waterfowl individuals_ in winter and spring relative to fall and summer? In other words, which species are _more abundant_ in winter and spring relative to fall and summer?  

For each question, _be specific_ about the calculated values from your object in part b OR visual components from your figure in part c that justifies each claim.

## 3. Waterfowl temporal dynamics

In the "Temporal dynamics of individual counts" section, we calculated the total counts within eBird groups.  

In this question, you will calculate and visualize the total counts within species for _Waterfowl_ to answer the question: What are the dynamics through time of total counts for individual Waterfowl species?

### a. Prep your data

You should be able to copy-paste the code from the "Temporal dynamics of individual counts" section and modify it to wrangle the data for Waterfowl. 

On each line that you modify, write in your annotation _how_ you modified the code.  

You should end up with a wrangled data frame that is ready to be plotted.

### b. Make a new figure

Make a visualization of the total counts for individual Waterfowl species through time. All species should be shown on your plot.

Additionally, for full credit:  

- label the x-axis, y-axis, and legend
- include a title
- use custom colors from a palette package or otherwise
- use a different theme from the default

### c. Interpret the figure

In 1-2 sentences each, summarize:  

- the similarities or differences between the figure you made in part a and the figure you made in part b. _Be specific_ regarding the visual components (geometries) and how they represent variables (i.e. the x- and y-axes and colors)
- 1 species that was consistently present through time in _high abundance_, with information from the visual components from the figure


## 4. New World Sparrow temporal dynamics

In this question, you will calculate and visualize the total counts within species for _New World Sparrows_ to answer the question: What are the dynamics through time of total counts for individual sparrow species species?

### a. Prep your data

You should be able to copy-paste the code from the "Temporal dynamics of individual counts" section and modify it to wrangle the data for sparrows. 

On each line that you modify, write in your annotation _how_ you modified the code.  

You should end up with a wrangled data frame that is ready to be plotted.


### b. Make a new figure

Make a visualization of the total counts for individual sparrow species through time. All species should be shown on your plot.

Additionally, for full credit:  

- label the x-axis, y-axis, and legend
- include a title
- use custom colors from a palette package or otherwise
- use a different theme from the default

### c. Interpret the figure

In 1-2 sentences each, describe:  

- 2 species that tend to be consistently present throughout the year
- 2 species that are only present in specific seasons (i.e. leave after a certain point in the year)

# Assignment checklist

Your rendered PDF should:

- [ ] include your name, title, and submission date
- [ ] include all code with annotations (see "General formatting components" for details)   
- [ ] be organized and readable (see "General formatting components" for details)     
- [ ] be free of messages and warnings from code chunk evaluation

Your responses should include:  

for Problem 1:   

- [ ] annotated code for Problem 1a
- [ ] annotated code and output for Problem 1b
- [ ] written response for Problem 1c

for Problem 2:  

- [ ] written response for Problem 2a
- [ ] written response for Problem 2b
- [ ] annotated code for Problem 2c
- [ ] annotated code and output for Problem 2d
- [ ] written responses for Problem 2e

for Problem 3:  

- [ ] annotated code for Problem 3a
- [ ] annotated code and output for Problem 3b
- [ ] written response for Problem 3c

for Problem 4:  

- [ ] annotated code for Problem 4a
- [ ] annotated code and output for Problem 4b
- [ ] written response for Problem 4c

Lastly, check out the **rubric on Canvas** to see the component breakdown in more detail.  

Your final submission should include:  

- [ ] a single PDF rendered from a .qmd file
- [ ] your submitted [Generative AI statement of use](https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSevf1zSjndFN-XMyKrCmpiIl-K6pwP4aUShOzI94t0ZW-yiuw/viewform) by the due date

# General formatting components

You will only receive full marks for annotations if you have:  

- meaningful comments on each line of visualization code and/or ggplot geom/theme call (not needed for each argument, though good to have)  
- meaningful comments on each function in any piping operations  
- meaningful comments in set up code to denote where packages and/or data are read in

You will only receive full marks for readability if:  

- code includes carriage returns, spaces, etc. to make pipe operations and arguments clear  
- code includes consistent spacing and indents
- headers are used correctly throughout document
- text is formatted correctly (for example, superscripts/subscripts appear correctly)
- text is distinct from in-line code or references to functions/objects (e.g. using backticks to denote functions)
